user_query:
Please build a small event-triggered automation for my baby-proofing practice that watches input/inbox for new home assessment files and generates client-ready summaries and email drafts. Use the provided automation_config.yaml and hazard_guidelines.json. On startup, perform an initial scan of any existing .csv or .html files in input/inbox (the included harper_family_assessment.csv and patel_family_assessment.html), then continue watching for new or modified .csv or .html files dropped into that directory.

Requirements:
1) Read local files and extract relevant content
- For .csv files like input/inbox/harper_family_assessment.csv, parse columns: client_name, email, address, room, hazard_category, severity, notes.
- For .html files like input/inbox/patel_family_assessment.html, extract client_name, email, and address from the #client section, and extract hazards from the table#hazards with columns Room, Hazard Category, Severity, Notes.
- Read automation_config.yaml to get: severity_threshold, top_n, and all output paths; read hazard_guidelines.json for mapping hazard_category to a recommended action (use the "default" recommendation if a category is missing).

2) Normalize, filter, sort, and rank records
- Normalize each assessment into a single JSON object with: client_name, email, address, and hazards (array of objects with room, hazard_category, severity as an integer, notes, and rank).
- Filter hazards for "top hazards" by severity >= severity_threshold from automation_config.yaml.
- Sort hazards in descending order by severity and then ascending by hazard_category for tie-breaking. Assign rank as 1..N according to this order.

3) Generate outputs for each processed assessment (base name = input file name without extension)
- Write a normalized JSON to output/normalized/<base>.json containing all hazards with their rank.
- Write a top hazards CSV to output/top_hazards/<base>_top<top_n>.csv with columns: rank,room,hazard_category,severity,recommendation. Only include hazards meeting the threshold, in sorted order, up to top_n (or fewer if not enough meet the threshold). The recommendation must come from hazard_guidelines.json or use the "default" text.
- Draft a plain-text email to output/emails/<base>.txt with at least: a first line "To: <email>", second line "Subject: Your baby-proofing assessment summary", a greeting with the client_name, and a list of the top hazards in the format "- <room> - <hazard_category> (Severity <n>): <recommendation>", followed by a brief closing (e.g., "Best regards, Your childproofing specialist").

4) Aggregate and log
- Maintain a consolidated index CSV at output/index.csv with a header row and one row per processed assessment including: assessment_file (base name), client_name, email, address, highest_severity (max severity across all hazards), high_risk_count (number of hazards with severity >= severity_threshold), and last_processed (ISO timestamp). Sort this file in descending order by highest_severity, then descending by high_risk_count, then ascending by client_name.
- Append a JSON line per processed file to output/log/processed.jsonl with: filename (including extension), client_name, processed_at (ISO timestamp), and hazard_count.

5) Event-triggered behavior
- The automation should process any .csv or .html files present in input/inbox on startup and then watch for new or modified .csv or .html files in that directory. When a matching file appears or changes, parse and regenerate the corresponding outputs. Ignore non-.csv/.html files.

Deliverables to verify with the included sample files:
- output/normalized/harper_family_assessment.json and output/normalized/patel_family_assessment.json
- output/top_hazards/harper_family_assessment_top3.csv and output/top_hazards/patel_family_assessment_top3.csv (top_n=3 per config)
- output/emails/harper_family_assessment.txt and output/emails/patel_family_assessment.txt
- output/index.csv and output/log/processed.jsonl

Use only the provided local files for data and recommendations; do not call external services. The automation may be implemented in any language, but it must satisfy the folder watching behavior and produce the outputs exactly under the paths specified in automation_config.yaml. All file paths are relative to the workspace directory.
